1:
The verse's plain wording carries the chapter's one real difficulty, and the witnesses agree in solving it the same way. On its face, God's second appearance follows the moment 'when Solomon had finished the building of the house of the LORD, and the king's house.' But the grammatical-historical commentators show this cannot be pressed into a date. Ellicott lays out the arithmetic: by 1 Kings 6:38 and 1 Kings 7:1 the temple took seven years and the palace thirteen, and since the works were successive (compare 2 Chron. 8:1), the palace was not finished until thirteen years after the temple. To read this verse as fixing the vision would force us either to postpone the dedication thirteen years — which contradicts the express declaration of Josephus and is 'in itself infinitely improbable' — or to set a thirteen-year gap between Solomon's prayer and its answer, which Ellicott calls 'even more preposterous.'

The true key, he argues, lies in the variation at 2 Chron. 7:11: this opening notice is merely a summary of the whole building history of the preceding chapters, not a chronological marker for the vision that follows. Gill reaches the same conclusion from the other side, noting that the temple itself 'was done in seven years' and that the appearance is better placed the night after the dedication than deferred more than a decade.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the verse devotionally. God had already given a real answer to Solomon's prayer by the fire from heaven that consumed the sacrifices (2 Chron. 7:1); here he adds a more express and distinct answer, appearing as he had at Gibeon in the beginning of the reign (2 Chron. 1:7). Henry therefore reads the two verses together: 'Solomon finished' must be understood as it is put in 2 Chron. 7:11, and the following clause as 'and the Lord had appeared,' the vision coming the very night after the solemnities of the festival.

The wesleyan Clarke gathers the chapter's purpose in advance: God appears to assure Solomon that his service is accepted, that he has taken the house for his dwelling, and that he will establish him and his seed on the throne of Israel for ever — but only on condition of upright obedience. The witnesses do not divide: this first verse is the evangelist-historian's recapitulating summary, and the God who hallows what men in sincerity devote to him now speaks his answer to the prayer of the dedication.
2:
The witnesses fasten on two words — 'the second time' — and on the timing they imply. The reformed Gill notes that this appearance 'was the second of the kind and manner,' answering to the first at Gibeon (1 Kings 3:5), and he presses the same chronological point as the previous verse: if the words are strictly joined to the notice of the finished buildings, the appearance must fall thirteen years after the temple was built, 'which is not probable.' Far more reasonable, he says, is that it came the night after the dedication, when an answer was returned to the prayer of the preceding chapter, for it is 'not reasonable to suppose' that so direct a reply should be deferred twelve or thirteen years. Gill even records the older solution of Junius and Tremellius, who read the whole clause parenthetically, 'for the Lord had appeared,' and of Piscator, who begins a fresh narrative, 'moreover the Lord appeared.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the substance of the vision and its bond with the prayer. God 'appeared unto him at Gibeon' before, and now answers the petition of the dedication point by point. The heart of the reply is, 'I have hallowed this house which thou hast built, to put my name there' — an expression drawn from Deut. 12:11. The sanctifying, they explain, consists in this, that Jehovah puts his name in the temple, filling it with the cloud that visibly displays his presence and consecrating it as the scene of the manifestation of his grace. To Solomon's request, 'that thine eyes may be open toward this house' (1 Kings 8:29), the Lord replies by 'always giving more than we ask.'

The wesleyan Clarke names the purpose plainly: the appearance, 'which was in a dream, as that was at Gibeon,' was to assure Solomon that God had accepted his service, had taken the house for his dwelling-place, and would establish him and his descendants on the throne of Israel for ever — provided they served him with an upright heart; but should they forsake him, he would abandon both them and his temple. The voices are wholly agreed: the second appearance is the gracious divine answer to a believing prayer, given without long delay, and it sets before the king at once a promise and a warning.
3:
God's opening word — 'I have heard thy prayer and thy supplication' — is read by the witnesses as the formal acceptance of the whole intercession of the previous chapter, and they concentrate on the great promise attached to it: 'I have hallowed this house.' The reformed Gill keeps it simple and pastoral: God heard the prayer recorded in the preceding chapter 'with delight and pleasure, and had accepted it.' The stress falls on the divine initiative — Solomon had dedicated the house, but it is God's prerogative to hallow it, to put his name there, and to fix upon it his eyes and his heart 'perpetually.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott does the careful work on the phrase 'for ever.' Its meaning, he insists, is determined by the prayer it answers: the words simply mark the temple as the 'settled habitation to abide in for ever' that Solomon had spoken of (1 Kings 8:13), in contrast to the movable tabernacle. Whether they carry any larger significance is expressly declared to depend on the faithfulness of Israel, as the verses that follow will show (1 Kings 9:7); and the clause 'mine eyes and mine heart shall be there' looks back to the same prayer (1 Kings 8:29). The 'for ever,' then, is real but conditional — a permanence of grace held out, not an unconditional guarantee.

Here the two readings quietly meet. Gill hears in 'mine eyes and mine heart' an unbroken, watchful love, and applies it beyond the building to persons: those whom God hallows and sets apart for himself 'have his eye, his heart, his love and care, and this perpetually.' Ellicott hears the same tenderness but binds it to covenant fidelity, so that the abiding presence promised in this verse is the very thing forfeited if the nation turns away. The one draws out the comfort, the other guards the condition; between them the verse stands as both assurance and summons. God has taken the house for his dwelling and answered the prayer made over it, yet the same breath that hallows it will, in the verses to come, warn that a hallowed house is no charm against apostasy — a house 'designed to protect them in their allegiance to God, but not in their rebellion or disobedience.'
4:
With the promise given, the covenant turns conditional, and the witnesses read the 'if' as the hinge of the whole passage. The reformed Gill fixes on the standard set before Solomon: he must walk 'as David thy father walked, in integrity of heart, and in uprightness.' David, Gill grants, was 'guilty of many sins and failings in life,' yet he was 'sincere and upright in the worship of God, never apostatized from it, or fell into idolatry' — and it is idolatry, 'what is chiefly respected,' that the condition guards against. The pattern is not sinless perfection but a heart that never turns to other gods.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the verse in its exact place in the dialogue. Verses 4 and 5, they observe, contain the special answer to Solomon's own petition (1 Kings 8:25), while the verses that follow (1 Kings 9:6) answer his prayer for the turning away of the curse. The demand is that Solomon walk before God 'in integrity of heart, and in uprightness, to do according to all that I have commanded thee,' keeping his statutes and his judgments — the language following the Pentateuch closely, and grounding the promise of the throne on the constancy of obedience.

The Matthew-Henry note carried over from the opening of the chapter presses the same nerve: 'that is the main matter — no religion without sincerity.' The establishment of the kingdom, on this reading, 'depended upon the constancy of his obedience,' for on that condition the promise had been made (Psalm 132:12). Here the witnesses are entirely united. Integrity of heart is the pivot; David is held out not as a flawless man but as a model of undivided worship; and the throne that God is about to promise 'for ever' is offered on terms. The verse is the great encouragement and the great warning of Solomon's reign in a single word — 'if' — and the tragedy of the chapters to come is that the son who heard it would not, in the end, keep the very sincerity his father kept.
5:
The promise now reaches its height: 'Then I will establish the throne of thy kingdom upon Israel for ever.' The witnesses weigh both the grandeur of the pledge and the shadow of its condition. The reformed Gill reads the 'for ever' with a Messianic horizon — the throne is to be established 'in a succession in his posterity, until the coming of the Messiah,' so that the everlasting kingdom promised to David is not finally broken but carried forward to Christ.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch print the whole covenant clause and its counterweight together, for verse 5 cannot be read apart from the threat that follows: 'as I promised to David thy father, saying, There shall not fail thee a man upon the throne of Israel' — immediately balanced by 'But if ye shall at all turn from following me.' The promise repeats the Davidic covenant, but the very next breath makes its enjoyment turn on obedience, so that the 'for ever' is held out to a house that may yet forfeit it.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott draws the sobering historical comment that the rest of the book supplies. 'The fall of the house of Solomon from dominion over all Israel,' he writes, 'is an emphatic comment on the conditional nature of this promise.' Yet he is careful to add that the essence of the covenant with David was still kept: even in the diminished kingdom of Judah, an unbroken succession of David's descendants was preserved — 'singularly contrasted with the changes of dynasty in the greater rival kingdom' of the north — and this, Scripture declares, was granted 'for David's sake' (1 Kings 11:13). So the witnesses hold two truths in one hand. The promise is genuinely conditional, and Solomon's line did lose the throne of all Israel; and yet the deeper pledge to David was not annulled, for God preserved the lamp of his house through every judgment. Gill's Messianic reading and Ellicott's historical one converge: the throne 'established for ever' outlives the failure of the kings who sat on it, because its permanence rests at last not on Solomon's obedience but on the faithfulness of the God who made the promise.
6:
The covenant's dark alternative is now spelled out, and with only one witness speaking at length the synthesis draws its weight from the verse's own words and its place in the argument. The reformed Gill weighs the Hebrew and finds the warning aimed not at a single lapse but at wholesale apostasy. 'If you shall at all turn from following me, you or your children' — the turning meant is 'not in a single instance, or in some small degree,' for the original, he notes, reads 'if in turning ye turn,' that is, if you 'utterly, and entirely, or wholly turn' from God and his worship 'to other gods.' The sin that would bring down the whole threat is defection to idolatry, the one thing David never did.

Gill is careful, too, about the breadth of the 'you.' The warning falls upon 'Solomon or his successors, or the people of Israel and their posterity' alike; kings and nation stand under the same terms. And the phrase 'turn from following me,' as the Targum renders it, means turning 'from my worship' — a settled abandonment of the covenant, not the ordinary failings of sincere men. The whole force of the sentence lies in that intensive doubling of the verb: God does not threaten to cast off his people for their remaining weakness, but for a decisive apostasy of the heart.

Read in its setting, the verse is the exact negative of the promise just given. Verse 5 had grounded the throne on Solomon's walking as David walked; verse 6 shows what would dissolve it — the going after and serving and worshipping of other gods. The condition, positive and negative together, is thus about worship before it is about anything else, which is why the historian of the book will trace Solomon's own downfall precisely to the strange gods his wives turned his heart to follow (1 Kings 11:4). The same covenant word that offers an everlasting throne warns, in the same breath, that a hallowed temple and a chosen dynasty are no protection to a people who forsake the LORD their God for idols. The house was built to guard their allegiance, not to license their rebellion.
7:
The threat reaches its climax: exile for the nation and abandonment for the temple. The witnesses read it as a prophecy the later history would fulfil to the letter. The reformed Gill takes 'then will I cut off Israel out of the land I have given them' as the plain forecast of captivity — God would 'suffer them to be carried captive into other lands, as the ten tribes were into Assyria, and the two tribes to Babylon,' a plucking up by the roots. The threat to the house, that God would 'cast it out of my sight,' means the temple itself abandoned and destroyed.

The wesleyan Clarke fastens on the second clause, 'a proverb and a by-word among all people,' and hears it echoing down to his own day. 'And so they are to the present,' he writes; 'the unbelieving Jews, the stubborn, stiff-necked Jews, are words still in common use.' They forsook the Lord, rejected his Christ, 'and are cast off, their temple destroyed, and they scattered over the face of the earth' — the standing, visible proof that the warning was no idle word.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the verse against the prophets who lived to see it come true. The same warnings, he notes, 'were repeated with terrible force by Jeremiah on the eve of their fulfilment' (Jer. 7:14; Jer. 24:9), who compared the coming ruin of the temple to the ruin that had fallen on Shiloh. And he draws out the strangeness of the threat's afterlife: 'the continued existence of the people, as a people, to be a proverb and a byword,' through the Babylonish captivity and their present dispersion, is a fact 'to which the history of the world undoubtedly furnishes no parallel.' Keil and Delitzsch confirm that the language is quarried straight from the Law, the threat resting on the curses of Deut. 28:37 and Deut. 29:24. The witnesses do not divide. Together they hear in this verse a covenant lawsuit spoken in advance: the God who has just promised an everlasting throne warns, in the very night of the temple's glory, that its height will only make its fall the more astonishing if the people exchange him for idols.
8:
The one obscure phrase in the threat — 'and at this house, which is high' — draws the witnesses into a small but real textual debate, even as they agree on its sense. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes that the relative 'which' 'is not in the original Hebrew here,' though it stands in the present text of the parallel (2 Chron. 7:21). The true meaning, he argues, is 'This house shall be high' — the reading of the Septuagint — while the Vulgate offers a good explanatory gloss, 'This house shall be for an example.' Either way there is an allusion to the temple's lofty position: generally, he observes, the exaltation of 'the mountain of the LORD' is made a type of its glory (Micah 4:1), but here of its destruction. 'Its magnificence and its ruin are equally conspicuous: for a city set on a hill cannot be hid.'

The reformed Gill reads 'high' first in dignity and then in elevation. Some render it 'the house of the most High'; it was a house 'in high esteem, fame, and glory,' built upon a high hill and itself of great height. The very grandeur in which Israel gloried, on this reading, is what the threat turns against them: the loftier the house, the more amazing its fall, and the more astonished the passers-by who ask, 'Why hath the LORD done thus unto this land, and to this house?'

That question, and its answer in the next verse, is where all the witnesses land. The height of the temple becomes a sermon in ruins. Matthew Henry's comment from the opening of the chapter had already caught the irony: the people 'prided themselves in the stateliness and magnificence of the structure,' but were to learn 'that it is not so high as to be out of the reach of God's judgments.' Whether the text reads 'shall be high' or 'which is high,' the meaning is one and the same. A building raised to honour God's name, if its worshippers forsake him, becomes instead a monument of his judgment — so conspicuous in its desolation that every traveller is made to hiss and to ask what sin could have brought so glorious a house so low.
9:
The threat closes with its own answer — the reason the ruin will be given — and the witnesses read it as the covenant's verdict pronounced in advance. The reformed Gill pictures the scene: it is those 'left in the land when others were carried captive,' the remnant 'capable of making the following answer,' who supply the explanation of the catastrophe. The desolation will not be mute; the survivors themselves will confess its cause — 'Because they forsook the LORD their God, who brought forth their fathers out of the land of Egypt, and have taken hold upon other gods.'

The wesleyan Clarke lingers over the vivid idiom 'have taken hold upon other gods.' Drawing on Eastern custom, he explains that 'when an indigent person claims the protection of a superior, he casts himself down before him, and lays hold of his feet'; to 'take hold' of a god is thus to fling oneself on it as suppliant and dependent. The image makes the apostasy concrete: Israel would abandon the God who redeemed them and clasp the feet of idols for the protection only he could give.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott draws out the terrible irony of the appeal to the Exodus. The bringing up 'out of the land of Egypt' is invoked here 'in exactly a converse sense' to its use in Solomon's prayer. There (1 Kings 8:51) the deliverance was the ground for pleading God's continued favour; here it is the ground of his claim to 'the undivided allegiance of the people,' for it marked his covenant with a nation now his own, and every covenant, he notes, 'involved reciprocal claims' (compare Jer. 31:32). The redemption that was Israel's comfort becomes, in their apostasy, the measure of their guilt. The witnesses converge on one solemn point: the destruction of the temple, when it comes, will carry its own explanation written on its face. Their sin, as Matthew Henry put it at the chapter's head, 'will be read in their punishment' — they deserted the temple, and therefore God deserted it; and the very Exodus that founded their privilege will testify against them when they take hold of other gods.
10:
The narrative now turns from the vision to affairs of state, and the witnesses first settle the chronology packed into 'at the end of twenty years.' The reformed Gill and the wesleyan Clarke give the same arithmetic: reckoning from when Solomon 'first began to build,' the temple took seven years and the king's house thirteen (1 Kings 7:1) — 'seven years and a half in building the temple,' Clarke specifies, 'and twelve years and a half in building the king's house' — in all twenty. The twenty years, then, cover the whole span of Solomon's two great works, temple and palace together.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch parse the syntax of the transaction. Verses 10 and 11 'form one period': the 'then he gave' of verse 11 is the apodosis to the 'and it came to pass' of verse 10, with the note about Hiram's supply of timber and gold inserted as a parenthesis. Hiram had furnished Solomon 'according to his desire' with cedar and cypress and gold, and Solomon in return, his buildings complete, gave him 'twenty cities in the land of Galil' — cities that, as the sequel will show, 'did not please Hiram.' They also flag the important supplement in the Chronicler, that these cities were afterward restored (2 Chron. 8:2).

Matthew Henry, whose fuller comment on this section opens at this verse, reads the exchange as the 'fair and friendly parting' of two kings when the work was done. Hiram had 'made good his bargain to the utmost,' so far 'from envying Solomon's growing greatness' that he helped to magnify him — for, Henry observes, 'God honours him; therefore Hiram will.' Beyond the food for Hiram's household agreed at the first (1 Kings 5:9), Solomon adds the gift of twenty cities, and Henry finds in the generosity a lesson: 'It becomes those that are great and good to be generous.' The witnesses do not dispute; between the grammarians' careful timeline and the preacher's warm portrait, the verse stands as the seam between the builder and the statesman, closing twenty years of labour and opening the account of Solomon's dealings with the wider world.
11:
The parenthesis about Hiram's help and the grant of the twenty cities occupies the witnesses with a legal and historical question: what exactly was Solomon giving, and by what right? The reformed Gill simply records the supply — Hiram 'had furnished Solomon with cedar trees and fir trees' for the building of both his houses (1 Kings 5:8).

The wesleyan Clarke presses the nature of the transaction and offers a shrewd reading. He doubts that Solomon alienated the cities outright: 'it is very likely that Solomon did not give those cities to Hiram so that they should be annexed to his Tyrian dominions, but rather gave him the produce of them till the money was paid.' For, he notes, the parallel says the cities 'which Hiram had restored to Solomon, Solomon built' and peopled with Israelites (2 Chron. 8:2) — so either Hiram never truly accepted them, or, having drawn their revenue until the debt was cleared, returned them. Clarke adds the suggestion that they may have been 'heathen cities which Solomon had conquered, and therefore had a right to give.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reaches the same conclusion about their status and the debt they discharged. The very act of giving cities 'implies a debt to him for timber and gold, and probably stone also,' over and above the payment in kind stipulated at the first (1 Kings 5:11). And since the Chronicler says that when the cities were restored Solomon 'rebuilt them, and peopled them with Israelites,' Ellicott infers 'they were previously cities of the subject races, which he would have no scruple in alienating' — though, he wryly observes, the law of Lev. 25:23 against selling the land 'would not have been likely to be strictly observed under his self-reliant despotism.' Keil and Delitzsch confirm the framing: Hiram had supported Solomon 'according to his desire,' and Solomon requited him after the buildings were done. The witnesses converge: the grant was payment, not a careless giveaway; the cities most likely lay on the Canaanite margin of the land; and the whole exchange was a settling of accounts between two allied crowns rather than a cession of Israel's inheritance.
12:
Hiram's inspection of his new cities and his displeasure occupy the witnesses with geography and with the likely cause of his discontent. The reformed Gill notes the simple proximity that made the visit easy: these cities, 'being in or near Galilee, were not far from Tyre,' so Hiram 'came out from Tyre to see the cities which Solomon had given him.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott supplies the fuller topography and, with it, a judgement about what actually troubled Hiram. The name 'Galilee,' he explains, means properly a 'circle' or 'ring' of territory, and is used in Joshua for a region round Kedesh-Naphtali (Josh. 20:7), lying to the north-west of the Lake of Gennesareth and extending to the Waters of Merom. 'The western portion of this territory would lie nearly on the frontiers of Tyre, and so would suit well the purpose both of Hiram and of Solomon.' From this Ellicott concludes that 'the discontent of Hiram probably referred to the condition of the cities' — which 'afterwards had to be rebuilt' — 'not to their geographical position.' The location was convenient; it was the state of the towns that disappointed him.

Matthew Henry, whose comment spans the whole episode, reaches for a gentler and more human explanation, unwilling to think either king behaved meanly. 'Did Solomon act meanly in giving Hiram what was not worth his acceptance? Or was Hiram humoursome and hard to please? I am willing to believe it was neither the one nor the other.' The country was truly valuable, Henry insists, 'but not agreeable to Hiram's genius': the Tyrians were merchants and seafarers, who 'knew not how to value a country that was fit for corn and pasture.' A land good for husbandry simply lay out of a trading nation's way. So the witnesses divide only in emphasis. Ellicott faults the fabric of the cities, Henry the temperament of the man who received them — but both agree the quarrel was no real breach between the kings, and that the episode ends, as the next verses show, in continued friendship and a return of the gift.
13:
Hiram's naming of the slighted region — 'the land of Cabul' — turns the witnesses into philologists, for the meaning of the word decides the tone of his complaint. The reformed Gill takes the words as sent 'by letter to him,' a formal expression of the Tyrian king's dissatisfaction with Solomon's gift.

The wesleyan Clarke lays out the uncertainty honestly. 'Whether this epithet was given to this land by Hiram as a mark of disapprobation, or what is its proper meaning, the learned are not agreed.' He notes that there was a real place of the name in the promised land in Joshua's time, part of the boundary of the tribe of Asher (Josh. 19:27), which leads some to interpret the word simply as 'border' or 'boundary' — the sense, he observes, that the Septuagint understood, rendering it by a word for 'border.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott gathers the threads and favours a play on words. The derivation, he grants, is uncertain, and Josephus 'evidently did not know it as a Hebrew word,' for he says expressly that in the Phoenician tongue it signifies 'what is unpleasing.' There is indeed a city Cabul in Asher, 'on the Tyrian frontier, and in the neighbourhood in question'; Ellicott's suggestion is that Hiram 'takes up this name, and applies it to the whole territory, and by a play of words on it signifies his discontent.' He records the proposed Hebrew derivations — 'as nought,' or 'like that which vanishes' — but cautions that unless they answer to 'something cognate in the Tyrian language, they hardly accord with the requirements of this passage,' which (as Josephus insists) implies a Phoenician origin. Clarke adds that there was such a country in the promised land already in Joshua's day, forming one boundary of the tribe of Asher, so that the word may bear a settled geographical sense quite apart from Hiram's mood, and 'the margin gives another meaning' still.

So the witnesses reach a shared, careful verdict. The precise etymology is lost, but the drift is clear enough: whatever its root, 'Cabul' became, on Hiram's tongue, a byword for a gift that fell short — a name that stuck 'unto this day,' and a small, wry monument to the one moment of coolness between two kings whose alliance otherwise held firm. That the historian preserves so slight a friction, in the midst of a chapter cataloguing Solomon's glory, is itself a mark of his candour: even at the height of the reign, not every gift pleased, and not every dealing between allies ran perfectly smooth.
14:
The note that 'Hiram sent to the king sixscore talents of gold' raises a puzzle of sequence and purpose, and the witnesses weigh both the vast sum and its place in the story. The reformed Gill reads it as gold Hiram had already advanced, not a fresh payment: it should be rendered 'had sent,' being 'the sum of the gold he furnished him with for the temple' — a hundred and twenty talents, which by the older reckonings he puts at a staggering figure, 'according to Brerewood, 540,000 pounds of our money.'

The wesleyan Clarke reads the flow of money the other way and connects it to the tax that follows. This, he says, 'was the sum which Hiram had lent, and in order to pay this Solomon had laid a tax upon his people, as we afterward learn' — the burden that would draw the elders' complaint under Rehoboam. 'The whole is very darkly expressed,' he admits, but the point stands: the gold and the levy of the next verse belong together.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott canvasses the possibilities most fully. The payment, 'on any calculation,' was large, 'though little more than a sixth of Solomon's yearly revenue' (1 Kings 10:14). How it fits the surrounding verses 'is matter of conjecture': it may refer back to verse 11, explaining the amount of gold Hiram had sent; or it may be 'a payment in acknowledgment of the cession of the cities.' Hiram's disparagement of the towns, Ellicott notes shrewdly, 'need not imply that he did not care to keep them,' quoting the proverb, 'It is naught, it is naught, saith the buyer: but when he is gone his way, then he boasteth' (Prov. 20:14). He even preserves Josephus's 'quaint story' of a contest in riddles between the two kings. Keil and Delitzsch, printing the verse with those around it, likewise treat it as the hinge into the account of Solomon's forced levy. The witnesses do not finally agree on the direction of the payment, but they converge on its weight and its function: a princely sum of gold that both seals the dealings with Tyre and sets the stage for the tribute Solomon must now raise from his own people.
15:
With 'this is the reason of the levy,' the historian turns to Solomon's public works, and the witnesses take up both the burden of the tax and the order of the building. The reformed Gill notes that the levy was 'both of men to work and of money to defray the expense,' the double conscription that made such projects possible.

The wesleyan Clarke ties the levy directly to the debt just mentioned: it was raised 'in order to pay Hiram the sixscore talents of gold which he had borrowed from him,' and it proved 'a grievous and oppressive tax,' as is clear from the elders' plea to Rehoboam that their heavy yoke be lightened (1 Kings 12:4). On Millo he follows the common view — 'a deep valley between Mount Sion and what was called the city of Jebus, which Solomon filled up.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott maps the fortress-cities and their strategic worth. The levy, first raised for the temple (1 Kings 5:13), was extended to the great building works. 'The Millo' — always with the article — seems to mean a 'fortified mound,' rendered by the Septuagint as 'the citadel' on Zion. Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer he marks as places 'all of important geographical positions,' and 'all had belonged to the subject races': Hazor in the north, commanding 'the great line of invasion through Hamath'; Megiddo in the plain of Jezreel, guarding the passes into the hill country; Gezer near the maritime plain, controlling passes 'of critical importance' toward the Philistine lowland (Josh. 16:3). Keil and Delitzsch add that these fortifications protected 'the mountainous country of Benjamin, Ephraim, and Judah' against invasion from the plains.

Matthew Henry, whose exposition runs through this whole section, draws the devotional order out of the list. Solomon 'built God's house first, and finished that before he began his own' — church before palace, palace before city — 'and then God blessed him, and he prospered in all his other buildings.' The lesson he presses is plain: 'If we begin with God, he will go on with us' (Matt. 6:33). The witnesses do not contend; the grammarians trace the map of a fortified kingdom and the preacher its moral, but all read the levy as the costly foundation of Solomon's magnificence — and a first, quiet hint of the discontent that would one day break his son's kingdom in two.
16:
A parenthesis explains how Gezer came to be Solomon's to fortify: 'Pharaoh king of Egypt had gone up, and taken Gezer, and burnt it with fire.' The witnesses fill in the history and the marriage-gift behind it. The reformed Gill notes the idiom of ascent — 'Egypt lay lower than Canaan, and therefore Pharaoh is said to go up to it' — and leaves the king's motive open: 'whether he went of himself provoked, or was moved to it by Solomon,' his son-in-law, cannot be said; but he took the place and burnt it.

The wesleyan Clarke supplies the town's chequered past. Gezer had been taken from the Canaanites by Joshua (Josh. 12:12) and assigned to Ephraim as a Levitical city, 'but it appears that the Canaanites had retaken it, and kept possession till the days of Solomon,' when Pharaoh 'retook it, and gave it to Solomon in dowry with his daughter.' The city that Israel had never fully subdued was thus restored to it by an Egyptian conquest and an Egyptian marriage.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the sequel: Pharaoh 'slew the Canaanites that dwelt in the city' and 'given it for a present unto his daughter, Solomon's wife.' As to why Egypt marched against it, they are candid — 'nothing is known concerning the occasion and object of Pharaoh's warlike expedition' — and they reject the guess that Gezer's people had raided the Egyptian coast, since the town lay 'very far inland.' Ellicott adds only the precise sense of the gift: 'a present — that is, of course, a dowry, on her marriage with Solomon.' The witnesses are wholly agreed. This verse is the hinge that made Gezer buildable: a Canaanite stronghold that had defied Israel for centuries fell at last to Pharaoh, and passed by way of a royal dowry into Solomon's hand, so that what the tribes could not conquer, a marriage alliance secured — and Solomon promptly rebuilt it as one of the fortresses of the previous verse. There is, in the plain notice, a quiet irony the witnesses leave the reader to feel: a city assigned to the Levites at the conquest (Josh. 21:21), long lost to the heathen it should have expelled, is handed back to Israel not by the sword of faith but by the arms of Egypt and the bond of a foreign marriage — the very kind of alliance that, multiplied, would in time draw Solomon's own heart away.
17:
The list of Solomon's building resumes with Gezer and Beth-horon the nether, and the witnesses treat it as a survey of a kingdom being fortified along its danger-lines. The reformed Gill notes only that Solomon 'built' Gezer in the sense of rebuilding it, 'it having been burnt, at least great part of it, by Pharaoh when he took it' in the previous verse.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw out the military logic. To 'build' here means not merely to raise but to fortify: Solomon fortified Lower Beth-horon, 'on the western slope of the mountains, four hours' journey from Gibeon,' and, as the Chronicler adds, Upper Beth-horon as well (2 Chron. 8:5), the two separated by a deep wady. 'The two Bethhorons and Gezer,' they explain, 'were very important places for the protection of the mountainous country of Benjamin, Ephraim, and Judah against hostile invasions from the Philistian plain' — just as Megiddo guarded the plain of Jezreel and Hazor the Syrian frontier.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds the sense of the name and the fame of the pass. Beth-horon, 'the house of caves,' was given to two small towns near Gezer, 'still called Beit-ur,' commanding 'the steep and rugged pass from the maritime plain' — a place 'celebrated for three great victories of Israel': the rout under Joshua (Josh. 10:11), the victory of Judas Maccabaeus, and 'the last victory of the Jews over the Roman army of Cestius Gallus, before the fall of Jerusalem.' The lower town 'stands on a low eminence on the edge of the plain.' Here the witnesses do not divide at all; they build up, between them, a single picture. Solomon's fortifications were not scattered by whim but planted with a strategist's eye on every avenue of invasion, so that this dry catalogue of rebuilt towns is in fact the blueprint of a secured realm — the peaceful reign of Solomon resting, like every peace, on well-chosen walls at the passes where armies must climb. Keil and Delitzsch underline the symmetry of the whole scheme: as the two Beth-horons and Gezer shielded the hill country from the Philistine plain, so Megiddo held the plain of Jezreel through which 'the high road from the western coast to the Jordan ran,' and Hazor stood as a border fortress against Syria in the north. The kingdom, in other words, was walled at each of its gates, and the name of the peaceful king was written, paradoxically, in a ring of frontier strongholds.
18:
The building-list moves to 'Baalath, and Tadmor in the wilderness,' and the witnesses divide their attention between the obscure Baalath and the famous desert city. The reformed Gill places Baalath briefly as 'a city in the tribe of Dan' (Josh. 19:44).

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm that location against wilder guesses. Baalath, being a city of Dan, was 'not far from Gezer,' and so 'is not to be identified with either Baalgad or Baalbek in Coele-syria.' On Tadmor they weigh the text carefully: the written form points to 'Tamar,' a palm-city, while the traditional reading 'Tadmor' (following 2 Chron. 8:4) is 'still the name for the city current among the Arabs even in the present day.' This, they note, 'the Greeks and Romans called Palmyra,' set 'in what is certainly now a very desolate oasis of the Syrian desert, on the caravan road between Damascus and the Euphrates,' where 'magnificent ruins' still attest its former glory.

The wesleyan Clarke seizes on Tadmor with evident wonder. It is 'almost universally allowed to be the same with the celebrated Palmyra,' whose ruins 'remain to the present day, and give us the highest idea of Solomon's splendor and magnificence.' Palmyra 'stood upon a fertile plain surrounded by a barren desert, having the river Euphrates on the east,' and Clarke lingers over the travellers' descriptions: 'The world never saw a more glorious city,' remarkable not only for its buildings and situation but for 'the extraordinary personages who once flourished there,' the renowned Zenobia and the incomparable Longinus. The witnesses do not contend; between the grammarians' textual care and Clarke's romance of the ruins, the verse opens a window on the reach of Solomon's kingdom — a caravan-city planted far out in the wilderness, a trading and garrison outpost stretching Israel's influence toward the Euphrates, and, in its later grandeur, a lasting monument to the king whose wisdom and wealth first raised it in the sand. Keil and Delitzsch note the purpose such a station served: set 'on the caravan road between Damascus and the Euphrates,' Tadmor secured the long overland trade-route through the desert, so that even this remotest of Solomon's works was no vanity but a link in the commerce that made his reign the wonder of the age — a wealth, the wider book reminds us, that could not by itself keep his heart, or his son's kingdom, from ruin.
19:
The catalogue ends with the store-cities, the chariot-cities, and 'that which Solomon desired to build,' and the witnesses read in it both the machinery of empire and the appetite of the builder-king. The reformed Gill glosses the 'cities of store' as those holding 'his magazines of corn, arms, and ammunition.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the system. The magazine-cities were 'fortified cities, in which the produce of the land was collected, partly for provisioning the army, and partly for the support of the rural population in times of distress' (2 Chron. 17:12), much like the store-cities Pharaoh had built in Goshen (Exod. 1:11); if set on the great commercial roads, they also served travellers. The cities for the war-chariots and cavalry, they add, were 'probably in part identical with the magazine-cities,' distributed through the kingdom, with some 'upon Lebanon' to secure the northern frontier against the opponent rising at Damascus.

The wesleyan Clarke turns the magnificence into a warning about its cost. Though 'by the multitude and splendor of his buildings, Solomon must have added greatly to the magnificence of his reign,' yet 'however plenteous silver and gold were in his times, his subjects must have been greatly oppressed with the taxation necessary to defray such a vast public expenditure.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott catches the softer note in the last clause. 'That which Solomon desired to build' points beyond fortresses to pleasure: he refers it to the vineyards, gardens, and orchards, 'whatsoever mine eyes desired,' of Eccles. 2:4, and to the 'vivid pictures of the pleasure-gardens of Lebanon' in the Song (Song 4:8) — 'in contradistinction from the cities of commercial and military importance previously mentioned.' The witnesses together sketch a kingdom at its zenith: granaries and garrisons for war and dearth, chariot-towns for power, and, crowning it all, the gardens a rich king built simply because his eye desired them — magnificence that Clarke reminds us was purchased at a price the common people paid. There is, too, a foreshadowing in that phrase 'whatsoever mine eyes desired,' for the Preacher who looked back on all these works confessed that when he 'looked on all the works that my hands had wrought,' behold, 'all was vanity and vexation of spirit' (Eccles. 2:11). The store-cities provisioned a kingdom; the pleasure-gardens could not finally satisfy the king who planted them — a verdict the chapter's very abundance quietly prepares.
20:
The account of the workforce begins with the conquered peoples, and the witnesses read it as history, as law, and as the fulfilment of an ancient word. The reformed Gill identifies the labourers: 'all the people that were left of the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites,' the Canaanite remnant 'not destroyed in the times of Joshua, or since,' who 'dwelt in several cities of the land of Israel.' They 'were not of the children of Israel; not natives of the land,' though some, he allows, 'might be proselytes.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the levy in contrast with the treatment of Israel. On these survivors 'Solomon levied a tribute of bondservice unto this day,' whereas 'of the children of Israel did Solomon make no bondmen; but they were warriors, ministers, and civil and military officers.' The free Israelites served as men of war and as officers over the chariots and cavalry; the subject races bore the servile burden.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott traces the institution back before Solomon. 'This was probably not originated, but simply enforced and organised, by Solomon,' dating 'in theory at least, from the Conquest.' Its most notable earlier instance was the case of the Gibeonites (Josh. 9:21), with other traces in the days of the Judges (Judg. 1:28). Their subjection 'varied according to the strength or weakness of each age,' but under the organised monarchy 'it was fixed definitely and permanently,' though, like medieval serfship, it might differ in severity from time to time and place to place. Matthew Henry, expounding the whole section, drew the theological thread: in serving himself of their labour rather than destroying them, Solomon 'observed God's law' that bondmen should be of the heathen (Lev. 25:44) and 'fulfilled Noah's curse upon Canaan, A servant of servants shall he be unto his brethren' (Gen. 9:25). The witnesses converge: the corvee that raised Solomon's cities fell on the old inhabitants of the land, a standing arrangement rooted in the conquest, sanctioned by the Law, and read by the preachers as the long echo of a patriarch's word over Canaan.
21:
The verse dwells on the descendants of the unconquered Canaanites, 'whom the children of Israel also were not able utterly to destroy,' and the witnesses, few here, draw out both the fact and its meaning. The reformed Gill identifies them simply: 'the posterity of those left unsubdued in the times of Joshua,' the children who remained in the land after their fathers, and on whom Solomon 'levy a tribute of bondservice unto this day.'

The wesleyan Clarke draws out what that bond-service meant in practice: 'he made them do the most laborious part of the public works, the Israelites being generally exempt.' To sharpen the picture he reaches for a parallel from antiquity. When Sesostris, king of Egypt, returned from his wars, 'he caused temples to be built in all the cities of Egypt, but did not employ one Egyptian in the work, having built the whole by the hands of the captives which he had taken in his wars,' and set over each temple an inscription boasting that 'no native has labored in these.' Solomon, Clarke observes drily, 'might with propriety have placed a similar inscription on most of his works.'

Read in its context, the verse completes the arrangement of the two previous ones and quietly notes its cost. Israel's failure to drive out the Canaanites at the conquest — a failure Scripture records without flattery (Judg. 1:29) — becomes, generations later, the reservoir of forced labour that built the temple, the palace, and the fortress-cities. The 'unto this day' marks the institution as still standing when the book was written, a permanent underclass drawn from the land's old inhabitants. The witnesses do not moralise heavily; but the juxtaposition speaks for itself. The magnificence of Solomon's reign, which the chapter has been cataloguing city by city, rested in no small part on the sweat of a conquered people, and the historian's plain notice of their servitude keeps the glory honest — a splendour raised, like Sesostris's temples, on the backs of those the sword had spared but not set free. Gill adds the legal ground that softened the arrangement short of extermination: these survivors could be pressed into service precisely because Israel 'were not able utterly to destroy' them, and so, rather than slay a people now dwelling peaceably in the land, Solomon bound them to the public works — a bond-service that, whatever its hardship, stopped short of the older command of the ban.
22:
Over against the servitude of the Canaanites stands the freedom of Israel: 'of the children of Israel did Solomon make no bondmen.' The witnesses read the exemption as a matter of covenant law and of national dignity, while noting how far it held in fact. The reformed Gill grounds it in the Law: to enslave Israelites 'was contrary to the law; they might be hired servants, but not bond servants' (Lev. 25:39).

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch spell out the honourable stations Israel held instead. Solomon 'did not make Israelites into tributary slaves; but they were warriors, ministers, and civil and military officers' — the king's servants, the heads of his military and civil service, his adjutants, and the captains over his war-chariots and cavalry. Where the subject races bore the corvee, the free Israelites filled the ranks of the army and the offices of the crown.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott, however, adds a candid qualification. This exemption, 'however it may have continued in theory, must virtually have been set aside in the later days of Solomon,' as the grievance brought to Rehoboam shows (1 Kings 12:4). The Israelites are here 'described as occupying the position of a dominant race,' like 'free vassals under a feudal monarchy'; but 'as the absolute power of the king increased,' their condition 'might be removed from serfship more in name than in reality,' and later history and prophecy confirm that 'there was such a thing as serfship of the poor to the princes' (Jer. 34:9; Neh. 5:11). Matthew Henry, expounding the section, hears the nobler intent: by employing Israelites 'in the more creditable services' and making of them 'no bondmen, for they were God's freemen,' Solomon 'preserved the dignity and liberty of Israel and honoured their relation to God as a kingdom of priests.' The witnesses hold the ideal and the reality together: the Law reserved Israel from slavery and Solomon honoured it in form, yet the swelling despotism of his reign strained that liberty, sowing the discontent that would split the kingdom under his son. Ellicott reaches for a telling analogy: as the free Macedonians found their standing eroded 'in the later years of Alexander the Great, when his royalty passed into something like a true Oriental despotism,' and the subject races were even 'played off against them,' so Israel's privileged place could be hollowed out by the same drift toward absolute power. The letter of the Law stood; its spirit was being spent — and the reckoning would come.
23:
A brief administrative notice numbers the overseers of the works, and the witnesses reconcile it with an apparently different figure elsewhere. The reformed Gill glosses them plainly as 'the chief of the officers over Solomon's work' — the supervisors set 'in building the above houses and cities.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch print the verse in its run: 'These were the chief of the officers that were over Solomon's work, five hundred and fifty, which bare rule over the people that wrought in the work,' and they connect it forward to the notices that close the building account — the bringing up of Pharaoh's daughter and the building of the Millo — observing that these verses 'point back' to the unfinished conditions at the reign's beginning (1 Kings 3:1) and show how Solomon's works removed them.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott takes up the number itself, which seems to clash with the parallel. 'In 1 Kings 5:16 we read of just six times as many officers as those here mentioned over the workers for the Temple.' His solution turns on the difference of occasion: 'in that passage there would seem to be reference to the special levy then raised' for the temple, a temporary and larger mobilisation, 'here the description is apparently of a regularly established system' — the standing administrative corps of the kingdom, not the extraordinary temple draft. The figures answer to two different things, and so do not contradict. The witnesses, few and agreed, leave the verse as a small window into the scale and order of Solomon's enterprise: a hierarchy of overseers, more than five hundred at the top rank alone, governing a vast conscripted workforce. Behind the poetry of gold and cedar in the earlier chapters lies this prose of organisation — the disciplined machinery of officers and levies without which no temple rises — and the historian's precise tally is itself a testimony to the sober competence that undergirded the splendour of the reign. Keil and Delitzsch note that these overseers 'bare rule over the people that wrought in the work,' a chain of command reaching down from the crown to the conscripted labourer; and the exactness of the number, like the exactness of the building measurements in the earlier chapters, marks a chronicler who means his readers to see not a legend but a real and governed kingdom, ordered from top to bottom for the great work it had in hand.
24:
The removal of Pharaoh's daughter 'out of the city of David unto her house' is read by the witnesses as a deliberate act of reverence for holiness, tied to the building of the Millo. The reformed Gill gives the reason most fully. Solomon had lodged her in the city of David 'when he first married her, until he had finished his buildings'; but when her own house was ready he brought her from thence — 'not only because it was the house of David, but because it was holy by the ark being there for some time.' He 'did not judge it proper that his wife, an Egyptian woman, and sometimes in her impurity, should dwell there.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott confirms the motive from the Chronicler, who supplies Solomon's own words: 'My wife shall not dwell in the house of David king of Israel, because the places are holy whereunto the ark of the LORD hath come' (2 Chron. 8:11). And he links the withdrawal to the works of the chapter: it is 'evidently connected with the building of the Millo' (1 Kings 9:15), which 'perhaps trenched on her former quarters in the city of David,' so that the fortification and the queen's move belong to the same programme.

Keil and Delitzsch add the topographical touch that the text itself implies. Pharaoh's daughter 'came up out of the city of David' — 'came up,' they note, 'inasmuch as the palace stood upon the loftier summit of Zion' — and these closing verses point back to the incongruities of the reign's beginning (1 Kings 3:1) to show how the buildings resolved them. The witnesses do not divide. Together they read a small domestic notice as a matter of principle: the king who had raised a house for God's name would not let even his royal Egyptian bride dwell where the ark had rested, and the very ground once hallowed by the ark's presence was, by her removal, kept from common use. Behind the political marriage stands a scruple for the holy — a scruple the same king would tragically fail to keep when foreign wives later turned his heart.
25:
The building account closes with a note of worship: 'three times in a year did Solomon offer burnt offerings and peace offerings.' The witnesses identify the occasions, guard the king's place at the altar, and draw the devotional lesson. The reformed Gill names the times — 'the feasts of passover, pentecost, and tabernacles' — and is careful to add that 'these were not the only offerings, or these the only times he offered,' for Solomon sacrificed also 'on sabbaths and new moons' and all else the Law required. The altar in view is 'the brasen altar, the altar of burnt offering,' the offerings made 'by whom,' that is, by the priests.

The wesleyan Clarke gives the three feasts in the same order, plainly: 'the passover; the feast of pentecost; the feast of tabernacles.' The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch add that with the temple now finished, 'the practice of sacrificing upon the altars of the high places could be brought to an end' (1 Kings 3:2); the royal offerings were now made 'before the porch,' and the closing words, 'so he finished the house,' set this act of worship as the seal on the whole enterprise.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the verse as 'a kind of note or postscript' to the record of the temple's consecration: to the great inaugural sacrifice it adds 'the solemn renewal of the royal offering, both of victims and of incense, three times in a year.' And he presses, with the others, the point that Solomon did not usurp the priest's office — 'there is no reason to suppose that on these occasions, or on any others, Solomon personally usurped the priest's office' (2 Chron. 8:12). Matthew Henry, closing his exposition, draws the heart of it out: the king 'took care to build' the altar, and then 'himself made use of it,' for 'many will assist the devotions of others that neglect their own.' The witnesses land together on a single, quiet truth: the crown of all Solomon's building was not the buildings but the worship they served — a king who, having raised the house, would not think the labour excused him from the altar, but made his own devotion the finishing of the work.
26:
The chapter turns from land to sea with Solomon's navy 'in Ezion-geber, which is beside Eloth, on the shore of the Red sea, in the land of Edom,' and the witnesses give their attention to the port, its name, and how Israel came to hold it. The reformed Gill notes the site as 'one of the stations of the Israelites, near the wilderness of Sin' (Num. 33:35), and passes on the tradition that the name, meaning 'the backbone of a man,' came from a ridge of rocks before the harbour lying in that form.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the fleet and the frontier. 'Solomon built a fleet at Ezion-geber, near Eloth, on the coast of the Red Sea, in the land of Edom,' a harbour 'at the north-eastern end of the Elanitic Gulf.' Eloth, 'literally, trees, a grove,' was named 'probably from the large palm-grove in the neighbourhood' (Deut. 2:8); it is 'the Aila and Aelana of the Greeks and Romans.' They note that Hiram 'sent shipmen that had knowledge of the sea' along with Solomon's servants.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses how the port fell to Israel and what it required. Ezion-geber, first named among the wilderness stations (Num. 33:35), lies 'at the head of the Gulf of Akabah, the nearest point of the Red Sea, on the edge of the mountain country of Edom.' Its very name, 'the giant's backbone,' 'indicates the nature of the country around it, which could hardly have itself supplied timber for ship-building' — so, he observes from the Chronicler, 'the ships, or the materials from which they were built, were sent from Tyre' (2 Chron. 8:18). The wesleyan Clarke adds a textual caution: the word is literally singular, 'a ship,' and though the parallel speaks of 'ships,' 'it does not appear that Solomon in this case built more than one ship,' manned chiefly by Tyrians. The witnesses converge on a striking picture: Israel, no seafaring people, comes into a harbour on the far southern gulf — a legacy of David's conquest of Edom — and there, with Phoenician timber and Phoenician sailors, the land-bound covenant nation reaches out, for once, toward the trade of distant seas.
27:
Hiram's contribution to the venture — 'shipmen that had knowledge of the sea' — draws the witnesses to a puzzle of geography and to the mutual dependence of the two kingdoms. The reformed Gill raises the difficulty frankly: how could Hiram 'send them from Tyre, which lay in the Mediterranean sea, to the above ports in the Red sea, without going a great way round?' He canvasses the conjectures — that Hiram kept a port on the Red Sea of his own for his eastern trade, from which he sent crews to Solomon's harbours, or the old speculations about a waterway joining the seas — and observes that 'this is the first navy of ships we read of,' in whose construction and navigation 'the Tyrians no doubt' were the masters.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch keep to the plain narrative: 'Hiram sent in the navy his servants, shipmen that had knowledge of the sea, with the servants of Solomon,' and 'they came to Ophir, and fetched from thence gold, four hundred and twenty talents.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott makes the verse a meditation on Israel's relation to the sea. The Tyrians 'were known far and wide as the great sailors both of the Mediterranean and the seas beyond it,' as Ezekiel's magnificent chapters on Tyre attest (Ezek. 27:8); 'the Israelites, on the contrary, had but little care for the sea, and little knowledge of seamanship.' Their coast 'is but scantily furnished with harbours,' and even at their height they were content 'to use the maritime skill of the Tyrians, without encroaching upon their commerce.' This, Ellicott insists, 'was natural; for their call to be a peculiar and separate people was absolutely incompatible with maritime enterprise' — so that Solomon's expedition was 'exceptional, departing from Israelite tradition,' the sea being mostly regarded in the Old Testament 'in its terrible power of wave and storm' (Ps. 107:23), and the new creation itself pictured with 'no more sea' (Rev. 21:1). The witnesses do not divide; whether they wrestle with the route or with the theology, they agree that the joint fleet displays two nations supplying each other's lack — Tyrian skill and Israelite gold — and that the venture, for all its splendour, stood a little outside the ordinary calling of the covenant people.
28:
The chapter ends with the fleet's return — 'they came to Ophir, and fetched from thence gold, four hundred and twenty talents' — and the witnesses spend their care on the great unsolved question of where Ophir was. The reformed Gill lays out the crowd of theories: some place it at Sofala in Africa, some at Ceylon, some in the East Indies, some even 'Peru in America' or Hispaniola, discovered by Columbus, 'who thought himself that he had found the land of Ophir, because of the quantity of gold in it.' The 'most reasonable opinion,' he judges, is a rich country in Malacca, the peninsula known of old as 'the golden Chersonese,' agreeing with Josephus.

The wesleyan Clarke confesses the honest limit of knowledge: 'No man knows certainly, to this day, where this Ophir was situated.' He notes two places of the name, one in India beyond the Ganges and one in Arabia near the Sabaeans, and recalls that 'the gold of Ophir' was proverbial for its fineness (Job 28:16). He follows Bruce in reasoning that the voyage required three years because of the monsoons, matching the note that Solomon's fleet returned once in three years (1 Kings 10:22), and inclines toward the coast of Sofala.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sifts the evidence most soberly. All that can be 'certainly gathered,' he says, is that Ophir 'was situated to the east of Palestine and approached by the Red Sea' (2 Chron. 8:18), and that its gold was so famous as to become proverbial (Ps. 45:9; Isa. 13:12). 'All else is matter of speculation and tradition.' Weighing Africa, Arabia, and India, he finds 'the preponderance of evidence' for India: tradition favours it, the Septuagint renders the name by the Coptic word for India, and the 'almug' wood associated with the trade 'is certainly the sandal wood found almost exclusively on the Malabar coast.' The witnesses, then, do not settle the map, and are content not to. They agree that the location is finally uncertain, that the weight of learning leans toward India or the far East, and that what the text means to record is not a place but a wonder: gold by the hundreds of talents, borne home from the ends of the earth to the king whose wisdom and wealth were now the marvel of the nations — the outward glory whose price, as the whole chapter has quietly shown, was paid in levies, servitude, and a fidelity Solomon would not, in the end, keep.
